content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
June 23, 2016 
Sarah Lang 
Bell, McAndrews & Hiltachk, LLP 
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 600 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Re: 
Your Request for lnfonnal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-16-053 
Dear Ms. Lang: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign reporting provisions 
of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 Because your question is general in nature, we arc treating 
your request as one for infonnal assistance.2 
QUESTION 
Under Section 84222 and Regulation 18419, when must a responsible officer for a 
multipurpose organization verify the campaign reports of a sponsored committee if the 
multipurpose organization does not meet the thresholds to qualify as a committee separately from 
the sponsored committee? 
CONCLUSION 
Generally, under each of the scenarios presented and assuming no other campaign activity, 
the sponsoring organization would not be required to file separate campaign statements from the 
sponsored committee or have a responsible officer verify the statements of the sponsored committee 
if the cumulative amount of all dues and all other contributions from the sponsor to the sponsored 
committee totaled less than$ 10,000 (the amount at which the sponsor would qualify as a major 
donor committee under Section 82013(c)) per calendar year. 
If the cumulative amount of dues and all other contributions from the sponsor to the 
sponsored committee total $10,000 or more per calendar year (qualifying the sponsor as a major 
donor committee under Section 82013( c)), a responsible officer of the sponsoring organization is 
required to verify the statements of the sponsored committee under either Regulations 18419(c) or 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sec1ions 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Go,·emment Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
 Informal assistance does not provide the requester with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation I 8329(c)(3 ).) 


File No. 1-16-053 
Page No. 2 
Section 84222(f) (whichever is applicable) unless the sponsoring organization files statements 
separately from the sponsor. However, we note that if the sponsoring organization qualifies as a 
recipient committee under Section 84222, the organization must file separately from the sponsored 
committee unless all contributions and expenditures are from funds derived from dues, assessments, 
fees, and similar payments that do not exceed $10,000 per calendar year from a single source. 
(Section 84222(t).) 
FACTS 
You have posed three common situations: 
• 
Situation 1: Sponsor is a membership organization and provides administrative costs to 
sponsored committee. Members pay dues, which are also collected by the sponsor, and a 
portion goes to the committee. As a result, the sponsor is reported as an intermediary. 
The sponsor does not make any general treasury contributions and administrative costs 
are less than $ I 0,000 per year. 
• 
Situation 2: Same facts as Situation 1, except the sponsor also makes a nomnonetary 
contribution of less than $10,000 to cover fundraising expenses in addition to 
administrative costs. 
• 
Situation 3: Sponsor is a membership organization and makes monetary contributions of 
less than $ I 0,000 only to its sponsored committee for the calendar year using non­
eannarked general treasury funds, but does not meet the dollar thresholds in 
84222(c)(5). 
Under each of these situations, you ask whether a responsible officer for the multipurpose 
organization is required to verify the campaign reports of its sponsored committee. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act defines a "committee" as "any person or combination of persons" who: 
"(a) Receives contributions totaling two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more in 
a calendar year; 
"(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) 
or more in a calendar year; or 
"(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more in a 
calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees." (Section 82013.) 
A "sponsored committee" is a committee, other than a candidate controlled committee that 
has one or more sponsors. Any person, (except a candidate, proponent, or other individual) may 
sponsor a committee. A person sponsors a committee if any of the following apply: 


File No. 1-16-053 
Page No. 3 
"(l) The committee receives 80 percent or more of its contributions either 
from the person or from the person's members, officers, employees or shareholders; 
"(2) The person collects contributions for the committee by use of payroll 
deductions or dues from its members, officers or employees; 
"(3) The person provides, alone or in combination with other organizations, 
all or nearly all of the administrative services for the committee; or 
"( 4) The person sets, alone or in combination with other organizations, the 
policies for soliciting contributions or making expenditures of committee funds. 
"(c) A sponsor that is a multipurpose organization, as defined in subdivision 
(a) of Section 84222, and that makes contributions or expenditures from its general 
treasury funds shall comply with Section 84222." (Section 82048.?(b) and (c); also 
see Regulation 18419.) 
Section 84222 establishes campaign reporting requirements for multipurpose organizations 
that are actively spending in state and local elections, defines when multipurpose organizations 
qualify as a committee for purposes of Section 82013(a), and requires multipurpose organizations 
qualifying und_er Section 82013(a) to report only their political expenditures and the sources of 
funds used for the political expcnditures.3 
With the exception of multipurpose organizations that are political committees registered 
with the Federal Election Commission or another state, Section 84222 permits a multipurpose 
organization qualifying as a committee under the section to report required information on its 
sponsored committee statement if the organization is a membership organization and "makes all of 
its contributions and expenditures from funds derived from the dues, assessments, fees, and similar 
payments that do not exceed ten thousand dollars ($10,000) per calendar year from a single source." 
(Section 84222(e)( 1) and (f).) Moreover, a multipurpose committee reporting on its sponsored 
committee statement must have both the responsible officer of the sponsor, and the treasurer of the 
sponsored committee, verify the statement. (Section 84222(f)(3).) 
Additionally, a multipurpose organization that does not qualify as a recipient committee 
pursuant to Sections 82013(a) and 84222(c) may still qualify as either an independent expenditure 
committee under Section 820 I 3(b) or major donor committee under Section 82013( c). (See Section 
84222(b).) In determining whether a sponsoring organization is either an independent expenditure 
or major donor committee, Regulation 18419( c) states that a sponsor that meets the thresholds set 
forth in Section 82013 is not a committee if: 
"(I) The sponsor does not directly or indirectly make or receive a sufficient 
amount of contributions or independent expenditures, other than those in support of 
its sponsored committee, to satisfy the thresholds set forth in [Section) 82013. A 
l Except as spec1fically provided in Section 84222 for multipurpose organizations, recipient committees 
established pursuant to Section 820 I 3(a) must report all contnbutions received and all expenditures made. (See Chapter 
4 of the Act, Sections 84100 through 84511.) 


File No. 1-16-053 
Page No. 4 
sponsoring organization makes contributions and expenditures in support of its 
sponsored committee when it provides the committee with member contributions or 
money from its treasury, with the exception of establishment or administrative costs 
(see [Regulation} 18215(c)(l6)); 
"(2) The sponsored committee reports all contributions and expenditures made 
in support of the committee by the sponsor, its intennediate units, and the members of 
such entities. With respect to a member contribution which is channeled through the 
sponsor or an intermediate unit, the member is the contributor; 
"(3) The sponsored committee reports as an intennediary the sponsor and, if 
required by paragraph (f) of this regulation, any intennediate unit, as an intermediary, 
if the sponsor or intermediate unit directly or indirectly provides the committee with 
$100 or more in member contributions regardless of whether any member for whom 
the sponsor or intermediate unit acts contributed $100 or more; and 
(4) A responsible officer of the sponsor, as well as the treasurer of the 
sponsored committee, verifies the committee's campaign statement pursuant to 
Government Code section 81004." 
As pertinent to your question, Regulation l 84 l 9(c)( l) clarifies that a "sponsoring 
organization makes co11tributio11s and expenditures in support of its sponsored committee when it 
provides the committee with member contributions or money from its treasury, with the exception 
of establishment or administrative costs." (Emphasis added.) Accordingly, to the extent that the 
dues and other contributions by the sponsoring organization to the sponsored committee are not 
reported pursuant to Regulation 18419(c), the dues and other contributions must be counted towards 
the $10,000 threshold for qualifying as a major donor committee under Section 820l 3(c). 
Under each of the scenarios presented, you have not specified the amount of dues provided 
to the sponsored committee by the sponsoring organization. Depending on the amount of these 
dues, the sponsoring organization may ultimately qualify as a recipient or major donor committee as 
follows: 
• 
If the cumulative amount of dues and all other contributions from the sponsor to the 
sponsored committee total more than $50,000 in a 12-month period or more than 
$ I 00,000 in a period of four consecutive calendar years, the sponsoring organization 
will qualify as a recipient committee under Section 84222(c)(5) with separate reporting 
requirements unless the dues and other contributions are reported under Section 84222(f) 
and the sponsored committee's statement is verified by the responsible officer of the 
sponsor.4 
4 However, we note that Section 84222(0 applies only if all contributions and expenditures are made from 
funds derived from dues, assessments, fees, and similar payments that do not exceed S 10,000 from a single source and 
the sponsored commillee's statement is ,·erified by the responsible officer of the sponsor. lfthe sponsoring committee 
qualifies as a recipient committee under Section 84222(c) and contributions or expenditures made are derived from any 
other funds or exceed S 10,000 from any single source, the sponsoring organization will have to file statements as a 
recipient committee separately from the sponsored commitlee. 


File No. 1-16-053 
Page No. 5 
• If the cumulative amount of dues and all other contributions from the sponsor to the 
sponsored committee total $50,000 or less in a 12 month period and S l 00,000 or less in 
a period of four consecutive calendar years, but $10,000 or more per calendar year, the 
sponsoring organization will qualify as a major donor committee under Section 
82013(c), with separate reporting requirements, unless the dues and other contributions 
are reported under Regulation 18419(c) and the sponsored committee's statement is 
verified by the responsible officer of the sponsor. 
In each of the scenarios you have posed, the sponsoring organization would not be required 
to file separate campaign statements from the sponsored committee or have a responsible officer 
verify the statements of the sponsored committee if the cumulative amount of all dues and all other 
contributions from the sponsor to the sponsored committee totaled less than the $10,000 per 
calendar year (the major donor threshold provided in Section 82013( c)). 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
( 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
Brian G. Lau 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Under Section 84222 and Regulation 18419, when must a responsible officer for a 
multipurpose organization verify the campaign reports of a sponsored committee if the 
multipurpose organization does not meet the thresholds to qualify as a committee separately from 
the sponsored committee?

If the cumulative amount of dues and all other contributions from the sponsor to the 
sponsored committee total $10,000 or more per calendar year (qualifying the sponsor as a major 
donor committee under Section 82013( c)), a responsible officer of the sponsoring organization is 
required to verify the statements of the sponsored committee under either Regulations 18419(c) or 
 Informal assistance does not provide the requester with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation I 8329(c)(3 ).)  
Section 84222(f) (whichever is applicable) unless the sponsoring organization files statements 
separately from the sponsor. However, we note that if the sponsoring organization qualifies as a 
recipient committee under Section 84222, the organization must file separately from the sponsored

(Section 84222(t).)

sections.analysis:
The Act defines a "committee" as "any person or combination of persons" who: 
"(a) Receives contributions totaling two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more in 
a calendar year; 
"(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) 
or more in a calendar year; or 
"(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more in a 
calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees." (Section 82013.) 
A "sponsored committee" is a committee, other than a candidate controlled committee that 
has one or more sponsors. Any person, (except a candidate, proponent, or other individual) may 
sponsor a committee. A person sponsors a committee if any of the following apply:  
"(l) The committee receives 80 percent or more of its contributions either 
from the person or from the person's members, officers, employees or shareholders; 
"(2) The person collects contributions for the committee by use of payroll 
deductions or dues from its members, officers or employees; 
"(3) The person provides, alone or in combination with other organizations, 
all or nearly all of the administrative services for the committee; or 
"( 4) The person sets, alone or in combination with other organizations, the 
policies for soliciting contributions or making expenditures of committee funds. 
"(c) A sponsor that is a multipurpose organization, as defined in subdivision 
(a) of Section 84222, and that makes contributions or expenditures from its general 
treasury funds shall comply with Section 84222." (Section 82048.?(b) and (c); also 
see Regulation 18419.) 
Section 84222 establishes campaign reporting requirements for multipurpose organizations 
that are actively spending in state and local elections, defines when multipurpose organizations 
qualify as a committee for purposes of Section 82013(a), and requires multipurpose organizations 
qualifying und_er Section 82013(a) to report only their political expenditures and the sources of 
funds used for the political expcnditures.3 
With the exception of multipurpose organizations that are political committees registered 
with the Federal Election Commission or another state, Section 84222 permits a multipurpose 
organization qualifying as a committee under the section to report required information on its 
sponsored committee statement if the organization is a membership organization and "makes all of 
its contributions and expenditures from funds derived from the dues, assessments, fees, and similar 
payments that do not exceed ten thousand dollars ($10,000) per calendar year from a single source." 
(Section 84222(e)( 1) and (f).) Moreover, a multipurpose committee reporting on its sponsored 
committee statement must have both the responsible officer of the sponsor, and the treasurer of the 
sponsored committee, verify the statement. (Section 84222(f)(3).) 
Additionally, a multipurpose organization that does not qualify as a recipient committee 
pursuant to Sections 82013(a) and 84222(c) may still qualify as either an independent expenditure 
committee under Section 820 I 3(b) or major donor committee under Section 82013( c). (See Section 
84222(b).) In determining whether a sponsoring organization is either an independent expenditure 
or major donor committee, Regulation 18419( c) states that a sponsor that meets the thresholds set 
forth in Section 82013 is not a committee if: 
"(I) The sponsor does not directly or indirectly make or receive a sufficient 
amount of contributions or independent expenditures, other than those in support of 
its sponsored committee, to satisfy the thresholds set forth in [Section) 82013. A 
l Except as spec1fically provided in Section 84222 for multipurpose organizations, recipient committees 
established pursuant to Section 820 I 3(a) must report all contnbutions received and all expenditures made. (See Chapter 
4 of the Act, Sections 84100 through 84511.)  
sponsoring organization makes contributions and expenditures in support of its 
sponsored committee when it provides the committee with member contributions or 
money from its treasury, with the exception of establishment or administrative costs 
(see [Regulation} 18215(c)(l6)); 
"(2) The sponsored committee reports all contributions and expenditures made 
in support of the committee by the sponsor, its intennediate units, and the members of 
such entities. With respect to a member contribution which is channeled through the 
sponsor or an intermediate unit, the member is the contributor; 
"(3) The sponsored committee reports as an intennediary the sponsor and, if 
required by paragraph (f) of this regulation, any intennediate unit, as an intermediary, 
if the sponsor or intermediate unit directly or indirectly provides the committee with 
$100 or more in member contributions regardless of whether any member for whom 
the sponsor or intermediate unit acts contributed $100 or more; and 
(4) A responsible officer of the sponsor, as well as the treasurer of the 
sponsored committee, verifies the committee's campaign statement pursuant to 
Government Code section 81004." 
As pertinent to your question, Regulation l 84 l 9(c)( l) clarifies that a "sponsoring 
organization makes co11tributio11s and expenditures in support of its sponsored committee when it

the $10,000 threshold for qualifying as a major donor committee under Section 820l 3(c). 
Under each of the scenarios presented, you have not specified the amount of dues provided 
to the sponsored committee by the sponsoring organization. Depending on the amount of these 
dues, the sponsoring organization may ultimately qualify as a recipient or major donor committee as 
follows: 
• 
If the cumulative amount of dues and all other contributions from the sponsor to the 
sponsored committee total more than $50,000 in a 12-month period or more than 
$ I 00,000 in a period of four consecutive calendar years, the sponsoring organization 
will qualify as a recipient committee under Section 84222(c)(5) with separate reporting 
requirements unless the dues and other contributions are reported under Section 84222(f) 
and the sponsored committee's statement is verified by the responsible officer of the 
sponsor.4 
4 However, we note that Section 84222(0 applies only if all contributions and expenditures are made from 
funds derived from dues, assessments, fees, and similar payments that do not exceed S 10,000 from a single source and 
the sponsored commillee's statement is ,·erified by the responsible officer of the sponsor. lfthe sponsoring committee 
qualifies as a recipient committee under Section 84222(c) and contributions or expenditures made are derived from any 
other funds or exceed S 10,000 from any single source, the sponsoring organization will have to file statements as a 
recipient committee separately from the sponsored commitlee.  
• If the cumulative amount of dues and all other contributions from the sponsor to the 
sponsored committee total $50,000 or less in a 12 month period and S l 00,000 or less in 
a period of four consecutive calendar years, but $10,000 or more per calendar year, the 
sponsoring organization will qualify as a major donor committee under Section 
82013(c), with separate reporting requirements, unless the dues and other contributions 
are reported under Regulation 18419(c) and the sponsored committee's statement is 
verified by the responsible officer of the sponsor. 
In each of the scenarios you have posed, the sponsoring organization would not be required 
to file separate campaign statements from the sponsored committee or have a responsible officer 
verify the statements of the sponsored committee if the cumulative amount of all dues and all other 
contributions from the sponsor to the sponsored committee totaled less than the $10,000 per 
calendar year (the major donor threshold provided in Section 82013( c)).

written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation I 8329(c)(3 ).)  
Section 84222(f) (whichever is applicable) unless the sponsoring organization files statements
